content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you have spent any time in backend web development, you’ve likely worked with the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). It powers Android, runs enterprise backends, and drives massive ecosystems like Scala and Kotlin. For decades, it has been a dominant player in server-side programming.
But if you look under the hood, you’ll find that developers have spent the last thirty years writing massive amounts of boilerplate, configuring hundreds of cryptic parameters, and building entire infrastructure frameworks just to protect themselves from the JVM’s fundamental architectural choices.
When you compare it to a virtual machine built for absolute resilience, such as BEAM (the Erlang/Elixir VM), you realize that for highly concurrent, low-latency, resilient systems, the JVM is fundamentally broken.
Let’s look at the JVM’s internals, why it struggles, how BEAM solved these problems decades ago, and why the industry adopted the JVM anyway.

1. The Genesis: Why Were the JVM and BEAM Built? 📂
To understand why these virtual machines behave so differently, we have to look at the historical problems they were designed to solve.
The JVM: “Write Once, Run Anywhere” (Enterprise Mobility) 🌐
In the mid-1990s, software development was a cross-platform nightmare. If you wrote an application in C or C++, you had to compile it specifically for every operating system (Windows, Solaris, macOS) and hardware architecture (x86, SPARC, PowerPC). Differences in compiler behavior, CPU instruction sets, and OS-level memory management meant porting code was slow, expensive, and extremely bug-prone.
Sun Microsystems solved this by introducing Java and the JVM with the slogan: “Write Once, Run Anywhere” (WORA).
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Instead of compiling code to native machine instructions directly, developers compile Java code into an intermediate format called bytecode (.class files). The JVM reads this bytecode, interprets it, and dynamically compiles hot paths to native machine code using a JIT compiler. To maximize execution speed, the JVM manages a single, large Shared Memory Heap where all application threads create and access objects.

The BEAM: “Keep It Running, Always” (Telecom Resilience) 📞
While Sun was tackling platform compatibility in the 90s, engineers at Ericsson (led by Joe Armstrong, Robert Virding, and Mike Williams) in the late 80s were facing a completely different monster: building telecommunication switches.
Unlike desktop or enterprise software, telephone systems had three non-negotiable requirements:

• Massive Concurrency: Handling millions of simultaneous phone calls.

• Soft Real-Time Latency: If the VM paused to perform housekeeping for even a fraction of a second, active phone calls would experience audio dropouts or disconnect.

• High Availability: Telecom switches cannot be turned off for updates or maintenance. If a bug crashes one part of the switchboard, the rest must continue running. Ericsson needed “five nines” of availability (99.999% uptime, or less than 5 minutes of downtime per year).
To solve this, they created Erlang and the BEAM VM (Bogdan’s Erlang Abstract Machine).
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Instead of treating the system as a single monolithic application, BEAM models it as a collection of millions of isolated, independent lines. Code is compiled into .beam bytecode files. Rather than running threads on a shared memory heap, the BEAM VM spawns millions of tiny, lightweight processes that communicate solely by copying messages. Each process is fully isolated, with its own private memory.

2. The Stop-the-World Janitor (Garbage Collection) 🧹
Because the JVM heap is shared across all application threads, managing memory cleanup is a challenging task.
When your application creates objects, they live on the shared heap. When they are no longer needed, a Garbage Collector (GC) runs to sweep them away and compact the remaining memory.
The JVM Problem:
To clean up memory safely, the GC must guarantee that threads aren’t modifying objects mid-sweep. To do this, it forces all application threads to pause at “safepoints.” This is known as a Stop-the-World (STW) pause. 🛑
Imagine a busy restaurant. The JVM’s GC is like a janitor who has to lock the front doors, freeze all the waiters and customers in place, and sweep the floor. No one can eat, order, or pay until the janitor is done.
Even with modern low-latency collectors like G1, ZGC, or Shenandoah, the JVM still struggles. Getting these pauses down to sub-milliseconds requires a complex puzzle of GC tuning. Developers spend careers tweaking flags like:
-XX:+UseG1GC -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=20 -XX:InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent=45The BEAM Contrast:
Erlang was built for telecom switches where latency spikes are unacceptable. If a switch freezes for 100ms, calls drop.
BEAM uses a Shared-Nothing (Actor Model) architecture. Instead of heavy threads sharing one heap, BEAM runs millions of lightweight, independent processes.

• Isolated Heaps: Each process has its own private stack and heap (starting at just ~2.5 KB). 📦

• Per-Process GC: Garbage collection happens on a per-process basis. If Process A runs out of memory, only Process A is paused to clean up. Processes B through Z continue running concurrently without a single millisecond of delay. ⚡

• Instant Reclaim: If a process completes its task (like handling a single HTTP request) and terminates, the BEAM instantly sweeps its entire heap away. No garbage collector ever runs for it.

3. Zero Fault Isolation: The OOM Blast Radius 💥
Because the JVM threads share everything, they have no boundaries. This creates a massive safety risk.
The JVM Problem:
If a single thread in your JVM application runs out of memory (e.g., trying to load a massive PDF or parsing a bad JSON), it triggers an OutOfMemoryError (OOM). Because the heap is shared, the entire JVM process crashes. 🤯
A single bad request can take down your payment processing thread, your database pool, and your healthcheck endpoints all at once. Furthermore, if a thread crashes due to an unhandled exception, it can leave shared objects in a corrupted, half-mutated state, causing other threads to fail silently or behave unpredictably.
To fight this, JVM developers have to write highly defensive code and rely on container orchestration:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

We configure Kubernetes to monitor the container, kill it when it crashes, and spin up a new one. In other words: we use Kubernetes as our global garbage collector. 🤖
The BEAM Contrast:
BEAM processes are fully isolated. They do not share memory and communicate solely by copying messages.

• If a BEAM process encounters an error, it simply crashes (“Let It Crash” philosophy). 📉

• It has no impact on other processes because it cannot corrupt shared memory.

• BEAM uses Supervision Trees. A supervisor process monitors workers. If a worker crashes, the supervisor restarts it from a clean, known-good state. 🛡️

4. Concurrency: OS Threads vs. Lightweight Processes 🧵
Handling millions of concurrent requests is the holy grail of modern web scale.
The JVM Problem:
Historically, the JVM mapped language threads 1-to-1 to OS-level threads. OS threads are heavy: they occupy about 1MB of stack memory each and require expensive context-switching managed by the OS kernel. This meant a JVM server could typically handle only a few thousand concurrent threads before running out of memory. 🐌
To bypass this limit, the Java ecosystem embraced Reactive Programming (WebFlux, RxJava). While it allowed higher concurrency, it forced developers into “callback hell,” made code extremely difficult to write, and turned stack traces into unreadable walls of text. 🌀
Note: While Java 21’s Virtual Threads (Project Loom) bring lightweight user-space threads to Java, they still share the same vulnerable heap and suffer from the same global GC overhead.
The BEAM Contrast:
BEAM processes are not OS threads. They are scheduled in user-space by the VM scheduler. You can easily spin up 2,000,000 Elixir processes on a standard laptop. The code remains simple, readable, and synchronous, without any of the reactive overhead. 🚀

5. The Warmup Penalty (The Cloud & Serverless Nightmare) 🥶
In the era of microservices and Serverless (FaaS like AWS Lambda), application startup time is critical.
The JVM Problem:
The JVM relies heavily on its JIT Compiler to be fast. At boot, the JVM is sluggish because it interprets bytecode. Only after running for a while and identifying “hot paths” does the JIT compile it to optimized native machine code.
This introduces two distinct performance penalties:

• Cold Starts: It takes seconds for a Java framework (like Spring Boot) to boot up.

• Warmup Sluggishness: The first few thousand requests sent to a newly scaled instance suffer from high latency while the JIT warms up. 🐢
To solve this, developers are forced to use tools like GraalVM Native Image to compile Java Ahead-of-Time (AOT). However, this removes dynamic class loading, makes compilation incredibly slow, and often requires complicated configurations.
The BEAM Contrast:
BEAM’s JIT compiler (introduced in OTP 24) is a “one-pass” compiler. It translates bytecode directly to native CPU instructions as soon as the module is loaded. There is no dynamic profiling or re-compilation.
As a result, BEAM has almost zero warmup period and starts up in milliseconds, executing code at peak performance from the very first request. ⚡

6. If BEAM is So Powerful, Why Didn’t It Win? 🏆
Given the architectural advantages of BEAM, why does Java run the world while Erlang and Elixir remain relatively niche?
1. The Familiarity Factor ✍️
In the 90s, most developers wrote C or C++. Java’s syntax was intentionally designed to look familiar to them. Erlang, on the other hand, had a Prolog-like syntax and forced a strict functional programming paradigm. To developers raised on object-oriented programming, Erlang looked like alien code.
2. Corporate Backing and Enterprise Marketing 💼
Sun Microsystems, and later Oracle, invested hundreds of millions of dollars marketing Java to enterprises. They built a massive ecosystem of certifications, university courses, and corporate partnerships. Erlang was open-sourced by Ericsson, but lacked a massive tech giant pushing it into corporate server rooms.
3. Raw Computational Speed 🏎️
The JVM is incredibly fast at heavy CPU computation, mathematical operations, and data processing. Because it compiles to native code based on runtime profiles, its raw math performance blows BEAM out of the water. If you are building Big Data pipelines (Hadoop, Spark), Machine Learning models, or physics engines, BEAM is the wrong choice. Its immutable memory model (which requires copying data) is too slow for heavy mutation tasks.
4. Ecosystem & Libraries 📚
Java has a library for everything. Whether you need to connect to an obscure legacy database, run PDF extraction, or parse XML schemas, there is a mature, battle-tested Java library available. While Elixir’s hex package manager has modernized the BEAM ecosystem, it is still a fraction of Maven Central’s size.

Summary: Choosing the Right Tool 🧪
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Conclusion 🏁
The JVM doesn’t “suck” because its creators were bad engineers. It sucks for low-latency, highly concurrent web systems because it was built as a general-purpose CPU execution engine optimized for raw mathematical throughput.
BEAM, on the other hand, was built as a communications system engine. It sacrificed raw mathematical speed to guarantee soft real-time predictability, isolation, and uptime.
The next time you find yourself configuring GC flags or setting up Kubernetes crash loops, remember: you are paying the tax for a shared-memory decision made in 1995.
